refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output to support evaluation against customer-defined energy efficiency targets.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the capability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a specific target value. The refined FR focuses on the functional requirement—providing visibility and traceability of energy performance—which is solution-neutral and within system control. Achievement of the target is a verification criterion (acceptance), not a functional requirement. The refined FR is atomic: it describes a single, measurable capability without 'and'/'or' conjunctions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution can continuously or periodically measure energy_used (kWh or equivalent) and work_output (tons moved or equivalent task units), compute energy_per_ton, and report this metric to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, Safety Manager, Maintenance/Quality) in a format suitable for comparison against E_target. Verify that measurement accuracy and reporting latency meet operational needs.
sub_frs: Measure total energy consumed by the NGHE solution during operation.
Measure total work output (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled, or task-specific units) during the same operational period.
Calculate energy efficiency metric (energy per unit output) from measured values.
Report energy efficiency metric to authorized stakeholders with sufficient frequency and accuracy for performance evaluation.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument work output (tonnage moved) and energy input (power consumed) at independent measurement points; aggregate and compute ratio (energy/ton) at a centralized analytics layer; compare against target threshold and generate compliance reports.

DP2: Deploy real-time energy metering on the power supply circuit and work-output sensors on the load/displacement mechanism; stream both signals to a local edge controller that calculates and logs energy_per_ton continuously; trigger alerts if threshold is exceeded.

DP3: Capture energy consumption via utility billing data (or sub-metered circuits) and correlate with independently logged work output records (tonnage, distance, time); perform post-hoc batch analysis to compute average energy_per_ton and validate against target.

DP4: Embed energy and work-output measurement logic within the primary control system; fuse sensor inputs, apply calibration factors, compute efficiency metrics in-silo, and expose results via a standardized reporting interface for external consumption.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Instrument work output (tonnage moved) and energy input (power consumed) at independent measurement points; aggregate and compute ratio (energy/ton) at a centralized analytics layer; compare against target threshold and generate compliance reports.

potential_sources: Sensor calibration drift: If energy and work-output sensors are not independently verified and maintained, measurement errors will propagate into the ratio and invalidate compliance claims.
Time-synchronization: Energy and work-output measurements must be temporally aligned; clock skew between independent measurement points could introduce systematic bias.
Aggregation boundary: The centralized analytics layer must clearly define the evaluation period (e.g., shift, week, month) and handle partial/incomplete data windows without bias.
Target threshold definition: Coupling between the DP and the acceptance criterion requires explicit agreement on how E_target is defined (e.g., per-shift, rolling average, peak vs. sustained).
coupling_check.notes: DP1 achieves logical independence by separating measurement (work output and energy input as distinct concerns) from analysis (ratio computation and reporting). This avoids embedding efficiency logic into the primary control system (DP4 risk) and allows independent validation of each measurement stream. However, the quality of the mapping depends critically on sensor independence, calibration discipline, and clear definition of the evaluation period. DP2 introduces tighter coupling to the control system and real-time alerting, which may be operationally desirable but complicates root-cause analysis if thresholds are exceeded. DP3 relies on post-hoc correlation and is vulnerable to data gaps and billing-cycle misalignment. DP4 couples measurement and control, increasing risk of systematic bias and making it harder to audit or replace the efficiency logic independently.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Work output (tonnage moved) can be measured independently of energy input without cross-contamination or feedback loops. Risk: If the measurement apparatus itself consumes significant energy or affects the load, the ratio will be biased.

Assumption: Energy consumption is attributable solely to the work output during the evaluation period. Risk: Idle power, auxiliary systems, and environmental factors (temperature, humidity) may inflate the denominator; acceptance criteria must account for baseline or normalized consumption.

Assumption: Sensors and measurement points are accessible and maintainable without disrupting operations. Risk: If calibration or replacement requires extended downtime, compliance verification may be deferred or skipped.

Assumption: The evaluation period is long enough to average out transient effects and short-term variability. Risk: If E_target is evaluated over a single shift or hour, operational variance may cause false failures; acceptance criteria should specify minimum aggregation window.

Assumption: Customer-defined E_target is stable and communicated clearly before the evaluation period begins. Risk: If the target is ambiguous (e.g., 'best effort' vs. contractual obligation) or changes mid-evaluation, compliance assessment becomes contentious.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not conflict with measurement instrumentation (e.g., no prohibition on sensor placement or data logging). Risk: If safety constraints restrict sensor access or data retention, measurement completeness may be compromised.

Assumption: The centralized analytics layer has sufficient data integrity and audit trail to support customer review and dispute resolution. Risk: If logs are not tamper-evident or are lost due to system failure, compliance claims cannot be independently verified.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): Enable independent measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output (energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved) over a defined evaluation period, with sufficient data integrity, calibration discipline, and temporal alignment to support objective validation against customer-defined energy efficiency targets (E_target) and resolution of compliance disputes. Acceptance criteria: (1) Measured energy_per_ton ≤ E_target, computed as the ratio of total energy (kWh) to total work output (tons) aggregated over the agreed evaluation period (minimum 1 week or 40 operational hours); (2) Energy and work-output measurement streams are independently calibrated, logged with timestamps, and reconciled for temporal alignment; (3) Measurement data and calculation methodology are documented and auditable by the customer; (4) Baseline or normalized energy consumption (idle, auxiliary systems) is explicitly accounted for or excluded from the ratio. DP1 is recommended because it enforces logical separation of concerns (measurement vs. analysis), supports independent validation, and allows the efficiency logic to be audited and updated without modifying the primary control system. Implementation modules (IM) should include: (IM-A) Energy measurement module (independent power meter or sub-metered circuit), (IM-B) Work-output measurement module (load cell, displacement sensor, or tonnage counter), (IM-C) Data aggregation and analytics module (centralized database or edge controller with time-sync and ratio computation), and (IM-D) Reporting and compliance module (dashboard, export, and audit trail). Each IM should have clear ownership, interface specifications, and calibration/maintenance procedures.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized analytics layer that aggregates telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems, computes energy efficiency metrics (energy/ton), compares against compliance thresholds, and generates compliance reports.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives work output (tonnage moved) and energy input (power consumed) measurements from Vehicle and Teleoperation modules at independent measurement points.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Transmits compliance violation alerts and threshold breach notifications when energy efficiency falls below target.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Supports data aggregation from distributed measurement points and report distribution to stakeholders.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment. NGHE Solution is explicitly positioned as the centralized analytics layer. Aggregation, ratio computation, threshold comparison, and report generation are all core analytics functions. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion and alert/report egress cross boundaries.

Poor containment. Vehicle is a physical asset responsible for work execution and local sensor data collection, not analytics. Placing aggregation and compliance logic here violates separation of concerns. High diffusion: analytics would leak into vehicle control logic and require bidirectional communication with Teleoperation and Safety Services.

Poor containment. Teleoperation manages remote control and operator interface, not system-wide analytics. Placing energy efficiency computation and compliance reporting here couples operator interface to backend analytics. High diffusion: would require cross-module dependencies on Vehicle telemetry and Safety Services for alert routing.

Weak containment. Safety Services is responsible for safety-critical functions and alerts, not energy efficiency analytics. While compliance reporting may trigger safety alerts, the core DP (measurement aggregation, ratio computation, threshold comparison) is not a safety function. Moderate diffusion: would require Safety Services to own non-safety analytics logic.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP is fundamentally an analytics and reporting function that requires a centralized vantage point across distributed measurement sources (Vehicle and Teleoperation). NGHE Solution, as the designated centralized analytics layer, is the natural and cohesive home for this capability. The DP exhibits high functional cohesion within NGHE Solution (all steps—aggregation, computation, comparison, reporting—are analytics operations) and minimal coupling (only standard telemetry ingestion and alert/report egress).
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle or Teleoperation modules lack standardized telemetry interfaces, integration complexity may increase. Mitigation: Ensure Telemetry interface is well-defined and enforced across all measurement points.

Risk: If compliance thresholds or report formats are frequently modified by Safety Services or external stakeholders, NGHE Solution may become a bottleneck. Mitigation: Design threshold and report templates as configurable parameters, not hardcoded logic.

Risk: If real-time compliance alerting is required (sub-second latency), centralized aggregation in NGHE Solution may introduce unacceptable latency. Mitigation: Consider edge analytics at Vehicle level for critical thresholds, with NGHE Solution handling post-hoc reporting and trend analysis.

Risk: Data volume from continuous power and tonnage measurements may overwhelm NGHE Solution if aggregation is not efficiently implemented. Mitigation: Implement data sampling, windowing, or streaming aggregation patterns.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 directly and completely addresses the FR. The DP specifies independent measurement of work output (tonnage) and energy input (power), centralized aggregation, ratio computation (energy/ton), threshold comparison, and compliance reporting. This logically satisfies the requirement to 'enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output' and 'support evaluation against customer-defined energy efficiency targets.' The DP is solution-agnostic and does not prematurely constrain implementation.

diffusion_risks: Moderate risk: Telemetry interface standardization. If Vehicle and Teleoperation modules do not expose consistent, well-defined telemetry contracts (schema, frequency, latency guarantees), NGHE Solution will face integration friction and potential data quality issues. Mitigation: Enforce Telemetry interface specification as a shared contract before implementation.

Moderate risk: Configuration coupling. If compliance thresholds (E_target) and report formats are hardcoded in NGHE Solution logic, changes driven by Safety Services or customer requirements will require code modification and redeployment. Mitigation: Externalize thresholds and templates as configuration parameters or policy objects.

Low-to-moderate risk: Real-time alerting latency. If SafetyAlert output requires sub-second response to threshold breaches, centralized aggregation in NGHE Solution may introduce unacceptable latency. Current design assumes post-aggregation alerting. Mitigation: If real-time safety-critical alerting is required, implement edge analytics at Vehicle level for critical thresholds, with NGHE Solution handling trend analysis and compliance reporting.

Low risk: Data volume and aggregation efficiency. Continuous power and tonnage streams may create high-volume telemetry. NGHE Solution must implement efficient aggregation (windowing, sampling, or streaming patterns) to avoid bottlenecks. Mitigation: Design aggregation pipeline with explicit data reduction strategies.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution exhibits excellent containment (0.92 score) for this DP. The centralized analytics layer is the natural and cohesive home for aggregation, ratio computation, threshold comparison, and report generation. All core analytics operations are functionally cohesive within NGHE Solution. Coupling is minimal and well-defined: inbound Telemetry interface (work output and energy input measurements) and outbound SafetyAlert and reporting interfaces. No analytics logic is scattered across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. The IM boundary is clear and defensible.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion 'energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved ≤ E_target over the agreed evaluation period' is directly measurable and verifiable. NGHE Solution's role as the centralized analytics layer provides a single, auditable point of computation and reporting. Measurement points are independent (Vehicle for tonnage, power subsystem for energy), reducing single-point-of-failure risk. Compliance reports generated by NGHE Solution can be validated against raw telemetry logs and customer-defined E_target thresholds.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to NGHE Solution as the owner of the DP. Vehicle and Teleoperation are allocated responsibility for accurate, independent measurement and telemetry transmission (supporting roles). Safety Services is allocated responsibility for consuming compliance alerts and integrating them into safety workflows (downstream consumer). Ownership boundaries are explicit and non-overlapping. No ambiguity regarding who computes, compares, and reports energy efficiency metrics.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Formalize the Telemetry interface contract. Define schema, measurement frequency, latency guarantees, and data quality expectations (e.g., precision of tonnage and power readings, handling of missing or stale data). This contract should be binding on Vehicle and Teleoperation modules to ensure NGHE Solution receives consistent, reliable inputs.

Revision 2: Externalize compliance thresholds and report templates. Store E_target and report format definitions as configuration parameters or policy objects external to NGHE Solution code. This decouples threshold updates from code changes and allows Safety Services or customers to adjust targets without redeployment.

Revision 3: Clarify real-time alerting requirements. If SafetyAlert must trigger within sub-second latency for safety-critical scenarios, document this explicitly and consider a two-tier architecture: edge analytics at Vehicle level for critical thresholds (e.g., immediate power anomalies) and NGHE Solution for post-hoc compliance reporting and trend analysis. If real-time alerting is not required, confirm that NGHE Solution's aggregation latency is acceptable for the use case.

Revision 4: Define aggregation window and data reduction strategy. Specify the time window over which energy_per_ton is computed (e.g., per shift, per job, per hour) and the sampling or windowing strategy to manage data volume. This ensures NGHE Solution can scale efficiently and that compliance evaluation is semantically clear (e.g., 'energy per ton per 8-hour shift').

Revision 5: Document audit and traceability requirements. Specify how raw telemetry, computed ratios, threshold comparisons, and compliance reports are logged and retained for audit purposes. This supports verification of the acceptance criterion and regulatory compliance.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP is logically sound, the IM boundary is well-contained (0.92 score), and verification is feasible. However, the gate results identify five concrete, actionable revisions that expose architectural dependencies and implementation risks that must be resolved before detailed design. Rather than revise at the current level (which would delay decomposition), we proceed to the next level with explicit sub-FRs that directly address the revision recommendations. This approach ensures that critical interface contracts, configuration externalization, latency requirements, aggregation strategy, and audit traceability are decomposed as first-class functional requirements, not deferred as implementation details. The high safety criticality of energy efficiency monitoring (risk factor: high) justifies this downward decomposition to ensure all safety-relevant concerns are explicitly captured and allocated.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Enable reliable, standardized telemetry ingestion from Vehicle and Teleoperation modules with defined schema, frequency, latency guarantees, and data quality validation.
FR1.2: Support externalized, dynamic configuration of energy efficiency compliance thresholds (E_target) and report templates without code modification or redeployment.

FR1.3: Determine and enforce appropriate latency and alerting strategy for energy efficiency threshold breaches, distinguishing between real-time safety-critical alerts and post-aggregation compliance reporting.
FR1.4: Aggregate energy and work output measurements over defined time windows with efficient data reduction (sampling, windowing, or streaming) to compute energy_per_ton ratios at scale.
FR1.5: Maintain complete audit trail and traceability of raw telemetry, computed ratios, threshold comparisons, and compliance reports for regulatory verification and forensic analysis.

revise.why: No revisions required at the current level. The FR, DP, and IM are sound. Decomposition to sub-FRs is the appropriate next step to operationalize the five recommended revisions as explicit functional requirements.

assumptions: Vehicle and Teleoperation modules can expose independent, consistent telemetry contracts for work output (tonnage) and energy input (power).
NGHE Solution has sufficient computational and storage capacity to aggregate continuous telemetry streams and compute compliance metrics in near-real-time (latency TBD by FR1.3).
Safety Services and customers can define and update energy efficiency thresholds (E_target) through configuration interfaces without requiring code changes.
Audit and traceability requirements are compatible with the aggregation and reporting latency constraints (to be clarified in FR1.3 and FR1.5).
The evaluation period for energy_per_ton compliance is well-defined (e.g., per shift, per job, per hour) and agreed upon with customers (to be formalized in FR1.4).
open_risks: Telemetry interface standardization: Vehicle and Teleoperation may expose inconsistent or poorly-defined measurement contracts, causing integration friction and data quality issues. Mitigation: FR1.1 explicitly decomposes this as a functional requirement.

Configuration coupling: If thresholds and report formats are hardcoded, changes will require code modification and redeployment. Mitigation: FR1.2 explicitly requires externalized, dynamic configuration.

Real-time alerting latency: If SafetyAlert must trigger within sub-second latency for safety-critical scenarios, centralized aggregation in NGHE Solution may be insufficient. Mitigation: FR1.3 explicitly decomposes latency and alerting strategy as a functional requirement.

Data volume and aggregation efficiency: Continuous telemetry streams may create bottlenecks if aggregation is not designed with explicit data reduction strategies. Mitigation: FR1.4 explicitly requires efficient aggregation with defined windowing and sampling.

Audit and traceability: If logging and retention of raw telemetry, computed ratios, and compliance reports is not designed upfront, regulatory verification and forensic analysis may be compromised. Mitigation: FR1.5 explicitly requires complete audit trail design.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface specification (schema, frequency, latency, precision, missing-data handling) agreed upon by Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution teams.
Configuration management design document specifying how E_target and report templates are stored, versioned, and updated without code changes.
Latency and alerting requirements document clarifying whether real-time safety-critical alerting is required and, if so, whether edge analytics at Vehicle level is necessary.
Aggregation window and data reduction strategy document specifying the time window for energy_per_ton computation and the sampling/windowing approach to manage data volume.
Audit and traceability design document specifying logging, retention, and forensic analysis requirements for raw telemetry, computed ratios, threshold comparisons, and compliance reports.
